title: Vigdis Hjorth’s Family Secrets
content:
Vigdis Hjorth, one of Norway's most famous novelists, is best known for writing about the dark, hidden traumas within families. She has published more than 20 books since the 1980s, many of which explore the concept of a woman trying to remember something dangerous. Her writing tends to be classified as 'reality fiction,' and for good reason. Hjorth makes Norway sound like a small town where neighbors know you are home just by seeing your footsteps in the snow. The frequent overlap between her life and work has more than once become the literary version of tabloid news.

In 2016, she published her critically acclaimed novel 'Will and Testament.' The book was a massive hit, won a major literary prize, but also made her family furious. Her sister Helga publicly accused Hjorth of using their family members' private correspondence in the book. In response, Hjorth simply stated that she had permission to use the material. Helga subsequently wrote her own novel about a woman whose sister lies about experiencing family abuse.

Unlike some of her fellow Norwegian writers, Hjorth deliberately avoids giving her characters real people's names. But if anyone had doubts concerning how close the fictional family came to reality, Helga's furious response seemed perfectly crafted to dispel them. Hjorth has suggested that the intense public speculation is partly her sister's fault. She noted that she cannot deny the book contains reality, because her sister's reaction practically proved it. This familial conflict highlights the ethical complexities inherent in the increasingly popular genre of autofiction.

Despite this controversy, readers should not dismiss her work as just another autofictional trauma plot. Hjorth's books, especially her latest novel, are much stranger and more intellectually interesting than that. As readers, we are accustomed to being steered toward the clear, explicit revelation of a trauma. However, what happened to the protagonist in her latest work is never made fully explicit. Instead, the book repeatedly returns to one long-ago November, leaving the reader slipping around in the dark.

What the novel is truly working toward is not the simple exposure of a violation. Rather, it seeks a deeper recognition of the self and the resilience required to survive childhood scourges. It portrays a storytelling-self who learns that fiction can ingeniously reveal otherwise unsayable truths. As the story opens, the narrator sits next to an unhappy teenager, vividly recalling events from her own youth. Ultimately, the narrative becomes a profound meditation on memory, survival, and the protective power of storytelling.
